items.title: Silicon Valley's Latest Binge-Watch Is a Humanoid Warehouse Worker - Business Insider
items.summary: It also exposed the gap between spectacle and commercial readiness: Figure's humanoids may be gettin
items.content:
It also exposed the gap between spectacle and commercial readiness: Figure's humanoids may be getting closer to human speed, but experts say they still have a long way to go before they can handle the messy reality of a logistics center.

## 'A whole new economy'

Many tasks that feel mindless to humans remain challenging for robots, requiring dexterity, perception, balance, and judgment people barely notice themselves using. That is part of what makes videos of humanoids doing routine work so compelling.

Last week, Figure AI released a video of two of its humanoids making a bed together, while French startup Genesis AI, backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, showed a robot cracking an egg and playing the piano. [...] Figure AI reached its goal of running the robot for eight hours with "zero failures," Adcock said, and decided to keep going. By the 24-hour mark on Thursday morning, the humanoids had sorted more than 30,000 packages, with more than 3 million cumulative views.

The viral stream is more than a robotics stunt. For Figure AI, a startup valued near $40 billion, it is a public audition for a future in which humanoids can work long shifts in warehouses, factories, and eventually homes. The demo gave investors and potential customers a rare look at whether the company's robots can perform repetitive labor reliably. [...] Subscribe   Newsletters

items.summary:
Congrats to everyone at Clio!

P.S. Stradley Ronon, an Am Law 200 firm, will roll out a firmwide dep
items.content:
Congrats to everyone at Clio!

P.S. Stradley Ronon, an Am Law 200 firm, will roll out a firmwide deployment of Vincent by Clio, following a multi-month evaluation of legal AI platforms. It matters as Clio is very much targeting the Big Law world now.

—

JAMS, the ADR service, has launched a ‘Technology Industry Group’, designed to address the growing volume and complexity of disputes stemming from rapid innovation across technology-driven industries.

—

Clarra, the case management platform, has announced a partnership and product integration with NetDocuments. The integration allows legal teams to access and manage NetDocuments workspaces and files directly from within the Clarra platform.

— [...] ## Artificial Lawyer

### Legal Tech & AI News and Views

# Claude For Legal, Clio $500m, Legal Innovators California +

15th May 2026 artificiallawyer Wrap 0

And another massive week for legal AI! But first, let’s hear it for our amazing Legal Innovators California sponsors. Below are many of the legal tech companies, ALSPs, and other businesses taking part in the landmark Bay Area event on June 10 and 11, San Francisco – at the heart of the world’s AI industry.

Come along to meet all of these great organisations and hear their experts. And there really is an incredible group here! [...] —

iManage has launched its MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server: a standardized, open-protocol connection that enables any AI systems to securely access governed iManage content without custom integrations, bulk data exports, or compromising existing security, ethical wall, and compliance controls.

—

And now two upcoming webinars on AI’s impact on legal education and training:

On May 26, 11:30 AM EDT, 4:30 PM BST, Litera in partnership with Artificial Lawyer will be holding a vital webinar on legal education and training in the age of legal AI. Speakers include Megan Ma, Stanford Law School; Ben Llinas, Linklaters; and Patrick Grant, The University of Law.

It’s free to attend. RS

items.title: Logistics Manager’s Index continues its upward climb, amid various issues and challenges - Logistics Management
items.summary: Those bright spots include things like improving freight volumes, carrier rate gains (driven largely
items.content:
Those bright spots include things like improving freight volumes, carrier rate gains (driven largely by reduced capacity rather than rising demand), and an industrial economy regaining its footing on the heels of an extensive downturn, in addition to other factors. Collectively, these things are helping drive up various key metrics by which logistics and supply chain operational performance are viewed and measured, which were highlighted in the new edition of the Logistics Manager’s Index, which was released earlier this month. [...] And while transportation prices are up, transportation capacity dimmed in April, coming in at 28.4, down 10.9% from March levels, with the 66.6% difference between the two metrics marking the largest on record, for the LMI.

Collectively, the report explained that the LMI has never previously become simultaneously tighter or more expensive.

“Freight markets were already on a strong upward trajectory coming into 2026,” the LMI stated. “The closure of the Strait of Hormuz and subsequent increase in fuel costs have supercharged these movements. While this is good news for carriers in the near-term, it remains unclear what the long-term effects will be.” [...] Air cargo remains a ‘shock absorber’ in turbulent trade

Tariffs, shifting trade flows and geopolitical volatility are pushing more shippers toward air freight—even as growth moderates...

Global Logistics: Europe recalibrates in a volatile trade landscape

Tariff uncertainty, shifting alliances and new trade agreements are reshaping supply chains across Europe as shippers and logistics...

# Chris Hayes Frames Emotional Survival During AI Boom

|

4.1

Relevance Score

Chris Hayes explores anxiety, automation, and strategies for emotional survival amid the AI boom, outlining personal coping approaches and emotional responses readers can use to manage rapid technological change.

## Scoring Rationale

A cultural/opinion piece about emotional coping amid automation, relevant for workforce and societal awareness but not a technical development, so moderate-low impact.

items.title: LaED: a novel lightweight, edge-aware and explainable deep learning model for privacy-preserving facial attendance tracking in resource-constrained educational environments - Nature
items.summary: The LaED framework consists of three interconnected layers: the Perception Layer for input validatio
items.content:
The LaED framework consists of three interconnected layers: the Perception Layer for input validation through rPPG-based liveness detection, deepfake screening, and preprocessing; the Identity Layer for facial representation learning, open-set recognition, and fairness calibration; and the Trust Layer for federated learning with differential privacy, tamper-evident audit logging, and consent management. Together, these layers support an edge-deployable attendance system with clear separation between sensing, decision-making, and governance.

## Methodology [...] In this paper, we present LaED, a lightweight, edge-aware, and explainable deep learning framework designed for privacy-preserving classroom attendance tracking in resource-constrained environments. The framework is motivated by real-world classroom conditions and education-specific biometric threat models, with the aim of enabling robust attendance verification while supporting fairness, transparency, and lawful data governance.

This work makes the following algorithmic contributions:

1. 1.

   Fairness-Aware Embedding Regularization: We propose a fairness-aware loss that constrains subgroup embedding centroids across age, gender, and skin tone to remain aligned with the global latent space during training, reducing demographic performance gaps at the representation level.
2. 2. [...] The resulting baseline fairness comparison is summarized in Table 9, which consolidates fairness gaps for traditional baselines, ArcFace, and state-of-the-art embedding-based methods (MagFace and AdaFace), alongside the proposed LaED framework. The fairness gap for ArcFace is derived directly from the subgroup accuracies reported in Table 8, whereas MagFace and AdaFace gaps are obtained from their respective baseline evaluations under the same fairness definition.

items.title: ShengShu unveils world action model to offer ‘infinite possibilities’ for robotic intelligence - Robotics & Automation News
items.summary: Motbrain learns from a far broader range of data than conventional AI models that train robots, incl
items.content:
Motbrain learns from a far broader range of data than conventional AI models that train robots, including unlabelled video, task recordings without language annotations, and data from different robot embodiments.

A proprietary latent action framework extracts physical motion directly from large-scale video, including human footage, simulation data, and multi-robot task trajectories, without requiring the data to be labelled or tagged to indicate specific actions.

This broader learning paradigm translates into strong scaling behavior. In task-scaling evaluations, Motubrain’s average success rate continued to rise as the number of training tasks increased, reaching approximately 92 percent at 50 tasks, while Pi-0.5 declined to roughly 68 percent over the same range.

items.title: Large language models require a new form of oversight: capability-based monitoring - Nature
items.summary: To address this, we propose a new capability-based monitoring framework that is better aligned with
items.content:
To address this, we propose a new capability-based monitoring framework that is better aligned with how LLMs are trained and used in practice. Capability-based monitoring is an organizing principle for generalist LLM oversight that tracks shared model functions across downstream tasks such as summarization or information retrieval, enabling cross-task detection of systemic weaknesses, long-tail errors, and emergent behaviors. We detail (1) a preliminary taxonomy of LLM capabilities, (2) monitoring dimensions and potential metrics, (3) implementation and governance implications of adopting this framework, and (4) future research directions to fully realize the potential of capability-based monitoring. We provide technical recommendations for methodologists and researchers alongside [...] ## The proposed framework: capabilities, dimensions, and metrics [...] Article

items.title: LaED: a novel lightweight, edge-aware and explainable deep learning model for privacy-preserving facial attendance tracking in resource-constrained educational environments - Nature
items.summary: In this paper, we present LaED, a lightweight, edge-aware, and explainable deep learning framework d
items.content:
In this paper, we present LaED, a lightweight, edge-aware, and explainable deep learning framework designed for privacy-preserving classroom attendance tracking in resource-constrained environments. The framework is motivated by real-world classroom conditions and education-specific biometric threat models, with the aim of enabling robust attendance verification while supporting fairness, transparency, and lawful data governance.

This work makes the following algorithmic contributions:

1. 1.

   Fairness-Aware Embedding Regularization: We propose a fairness-aware loss that constrains subgroup embedding centroids across age, gender, and skin tone to remain aligned with the global latent space during training, reducing demographic performance gaps at the representation level.
2. 2. [...] Before applying fairness calibration, the model exhibited accuracy disparities of 3.2 to 4.1% points across gender and skin tone subgroups. For example, darker-skinned female students achieved 92.3% accuracy compared to 95.5% for lighter-skinned males. After applying the demographic-parity regularizer, these gaps were reduced to less than 1%.

Accuracy for darker-skinned females increased from 92.3 to 96.5%, while accuracy for younger students improved from 92.4 to 96.1%. These results show that fairness calibration reduced demographic gaps by approximately 70% on average. Importantly, overall model accuracy remained stable at 97.8%, indicating that the fairness constraint did not introduce any measurable performance trade-off. [...] ## Conclusion and future work

This paper introduced LaED, a layered, lightweight, and explainable framework for classroom attendance tracking that goes beyond conventional face recognition pipelines. By integrating multimodal spoof and deepfake defenses, open-set recognition, fairness calibration, federated privacy-preserving learning, and tamper-evident auditability, LaED closes critical loopholes left by existing attendance systems.

The experimental

 CEO Brett Adcock said the robots worked autonomously with zero failures for 24 hours.
 One robotics expert said the demo was impressive, but the humanoids are not deployment-ready.

[...] Among the viewers of this week's livestream were Figure investors and board members Jesse Coors-Blankenship and Gregg Hill of Parkway Venture Capital, who said they were watching from their New York office and planned to celebrate the milestone later.

> Watch a team of humanoid robots running a full 8-hr shift at human performance levels. This is fully autonomous running Helix-02 
>
> — Brett Adcock (@adcock\_brett) May 13, 2026

Coors-Blankenship said the conveyor belt was a large loop, with the same packages cycling through repeatedly. The point, he said, was to show potential customers that Figure AI's humanoids can work reliably for long stretches, including 24-hour shifts. [...] Rya is a senior reporter at Business Insider covering physical AI and robotics. She writes about factory automation, humanoid robots, and the race to collect the real-world data needed to bring AI into the physical world. She previously worked at The San Francisco Standard, where she reported on tech culture and autonomous vehicles. She has a bachelor’s degree in history and politics from Pomona College and a master’s in history from the University of Cambridge. Rya lives in San Francisco. Privacy Policy

#### Strictly Necessary Cookies

Always Active [...] Some startups model psychological mechanisms that drive consumer choices; others, like Unbox.AI, train on transactional signals like clicks, scrolls and purchase sequences. What they share is the conviction that the distribution problem is partly an artifact of modeling what people say rather than what they do. When you build from behavioral data, the messy variance that language models flatten is preserved by design, and the intent-action gap, the oldest unsolved problem in consumer research, becomes structurally narrower.

### The Bigger Shift [...] # The Invisible Bias Problem in AI Consumer Research - Retail TouchPoints
